title: Debugging and AI-Assisted Development

learningObjectives.text: Identify and correct a repeatable debugging process using a new example and justify the result.
Identify and correct finding and isolating errors using a new example and justify the result.
Explain using ai tools without outsourcing understanding using a new example and justify the result.
learningObjectives.subtopic: Using AI tools without outsourcing understanding

whyThisMatters: This topic turns debugging and ai-assisted development from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Developer Skills** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **A repeatable debugging process** → **Finding and isolating errors** → **Using AI tools without outsourcing understanding**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: A repeatable debugging process
sections.body: Debugging is an evidence-driven loop: reproduce, narrow, inspect, hypothesize, change one thing, and verify. Random edits hide the cause and create new variables.

sections.title: Finding and isolating errors
sections.body: Use a minimal failing case, logs, breakpoints, stack traces, and binary isolation to locate where observed behavior diverges from expected behavior.

sections.title: Using AI tools without outsourcing understanding
sections.body: AI tools can accelerate explanation and review, but generated output is an untrusted proposal. Supply exact context, ask for assumptions, run the result, compare with documentation, and explain it independently.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: Evidence is recorded at the boundary and failure produces a precise error.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Debugging and AI-Assisted Development connects A repeatable debugging process, Finding and isolating errors, Using AI tools without outsourcing understanding.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

choices.label: Professional development includes reading documentation, forming a hypothesis, and running a small experiment. The goal is not to copy a result but to gather evidence that changes or confirms understanding.

choices.label: Capturing is the earlier phase from ancestors toward the target. Most application handlers use bubbling, but capture can observe an event first.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Debugging and AI-Assisted Development in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Apply A repeatable debugging process deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.”?

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Debugging and AI-Assisted Development?

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from A repeatable debugging process, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Debugging and AI-Assisted Development?

choices.label: Use Debugging and AI-Assisted Development when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use Debugging and AI-Assisted Development in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

choices.label: Avoid Debugging and AI-Assisted Development completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Debugging and AI-Assisted Development.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
